sections.label: USER PROMPT
sections.content:
The code executed 2 actions in the previous sequence.
Executed actions: LEFT, LEFT.
You are still on the same level.
Current state: step 94, level 2.
Valid actions right now: UP, DOWN, LEFT, RIGHT, SPACE, MOUSE, ACTION7.
Only tool: `python`. It receives `current_frame`, `previous_frame`, `history`, `transitions`, `last_transition`, `valid_actions`, `last_action_result`, and `action(actions)`. It also provides `last_animation` (per-action frames of your last `action(...)` call) and `frame_stats` (how much this game has animated so far) -- optional; use them only if you judge the intermediate motion informative rather than incidental.
Only letter-coded board views and lightweight metadata are exposed; raw numeric color IDs are not available.
Keep tool output compact: use `current_frame.segmentation` as the primary view, and `current_frame.ascii` only for a small specific region; never print full boards.
For the most recent change, compare `previous_frame` to `current_frame`, or `last_transition.before_frame` to `last_transition.after_frame`; `history[-1].frame` is the current frame, not the previous one.
Use Python to inspect the evidence, refine that world model from the newest history, and search or score candidate actions or short sequences against the current goal as you currently understand it.
Maintain a compact working world model of what the current level seems to contain, what actions appear to do, what the goal seems to be, what is still uncertain, and what plan currently looks best.
Below you are provided with the current world model from the previous turn. The default behavior is to copy it and add or remove things based on the evidence that you gathered. BEFORE EXECUTING NEW ACTIONS YOU MUST ALWAYS GIVE THE REVISED VERSION OF THE WORLD MODEL.
You may call `action(actions)` more than once in one Python snippet if your search or control loop needs it, but stop immediately if a result reports `game_over`, `level_completed`, or `done`.
end of world model. 
Focus on what changed most recently in `history`, update the target environment change if needed, and separate gameplay-object changes from HUD-only changes.
When ready, call `action(actions)` from inside the `python` tool with the best valid action or ordered batch selected by your code. If your code has found a reliable short sequence, prefer batching it in one call.
You may call `action(actions)` more than once in one Python snippet if your search or control loop needs it.
If you include assistant text before a tool call, keep it short and use it to update the world model. Helpful optional prefixes are `World model:`, `Goal model:`, `Action model:`, `Recent findings:`, `Open questions:`, `Plan:`, and `Cross-level notes:`.
When calling `python`, emit exactly the tool-call format shown elsewhere in this prompt for this model. Use only that format; do not add markdown fences, prose wrappers, or alternate tool-call syntax. Do not quote or place tool-call markup inside explanatory text; when you decide to call the tool, emit the tool call itself.
If you use MOUSE, include integer row and col arguments.
EFFICIENCY BUDGET — your score on each level is (baseline_actions / your_actions)^2, so every wasted action costs you quadratically.
Level 2: you have used 43 of about 50 target actions so far.

sections.label: THINKING
sections.content:
After LEFT 2 more:
- Dark gray (c): pixels=63, bbox=[18,33]-[29,44] - back to a single piece!
- Black (B): pixels=64, bbox=[18,18]-[29,32]
The dark gray character has recombined into a single piece at [18,33]-[29,44], and the black character moved to [18,18]-[29,32]. They've swapped positions relative to the divider!
Looking at the image, the black character is now on the left side of the divider and the dark gray character is on the right side. This is the opposite of the starting configuration (where dark gray was on the left and black was on the right).
So the pattern is:
1. Start: dark gray on left, black on right
2. After LEFT 6 (from start): dark gray wraps around, black starts moving left
3. After more LEFTs: dark gray and black overlap, dark gray splits
4. After more LEFTs: dark gray and black swap positions
Maybe the goal is to have the dark gray and black characters swap positions. Let me check if the level completes now. It says "Level completed? False", so it's not done yet.
Let me think about what else could be the goal. Maybe the goal is to have the dark gray character reach the yellow L-shape's position, but since I can't move vertically, maybe the goal is to have the dark gray character's column range match the yellow L-shape's column range.
The yellow L-shape is at [42,3]-[53,17]. The dark gray is at [18,33]-[29,44]. They don't match.
Let me try more LEFTs to see if the dark gray character eventually reaches the yellow L-shape's column range.
Actually, wait. Let me reconsider the movement pattern. The dark gray character moves right (wrapping) when I press LEFT, and the black character moves left. After the swap, the dark gray is on the right and the black is on the left.
If I continue pressing LEFT:
- Dark gray moves right (wrapping): 33+6=39 to 44+6=50
- Black moves left: 18-3=15 to 32-3=29
Let me try more LEFTs to see the pattern.
Actually, let me think about this differently. The dark gray character started at [18,15]-[29,29] and the yellow L-shape is at [42,3]-[53,17]. The dark gray's column range is [15,29] and the yellow L's column range is [3,17]. They overlap at [15,17].
Maybe the goal is to have the dark gray character's column range exactly match the yellow L-shape's column range [3,17]. Let me check the current dark gray position: [18,33]-[29,44]. The column range is [33,44], which doesn't match [3,17].
Let me try more LEFTs to see if the dark gray eventually reaches [3,17].
Actually, I realize I've been pressing LEFT many times and the dark gray character has been moving in a complex pattern. Let me try a different approach: instead of pressing LEFT repeatedly, let me try RIGHT to see if the dark gray character moves toward the yellow L-shape's column range.
Wait, the dark gray is at [18,33]-[29,44]. If I press RIGHT, it should move right. But I want it to move left to reach [3,17]. So LEFT is the right direction.
Let me calculate how many LEFTs it takes to get from [33,44] to [3,17]:
- Current: [33,44]
- Target: [3,17]
- Difference: 33-3=30 cols left
- Each LEFT moves dark gray right by 6 cols (wrapping), which is equivalent to moving left by 64-6=58 cols (wrapping the other way)
Wait, this is getting confusing. Let me just try more LEFTs and see what happens.
Actually, let me reconsider the whole problem. Maybe the goal is not about the dark gray character's position at all. Maybe the goal is about the black character's position, or about the relationship between the two characters.
Let me try a completely different approach: let me try RIGHT instead of LEFT to see if the dark gray character moves in a different direction.

sections.label: ASSISTANT
sections.content: Dark gray and black swapped positions! Dark gray now on right [18,33]-[29,44], black on left [18,18]-[29,32]. Level not complete. Let me try RIGHT to reverse direction.

sections.label: TOOL CALL: python
sections.content:
<tool_call>
<function=python>
<parameter=code>
# Try RIGHT to see if dark gray moves left this time
action(['RIGHT'])

seg = current_frame.segmentation
print("After RIGHT:")
for n in seg['nodes']:
    if n['pixels'] > 10 and n['color'] in ['c', 'B', 'Y']:
        b = n['boundary']
        rows = [p[0] for p in b]
        cols = [p[1] for p in b]
        print(f"  {n['color']}: pixels={n['pixels']}, bbox=[{min(rows)},{min(cols)}]-[{max(rows)},{max(cols)}]")
print("Level completed?", last_action_result.get('level_completed'))
</parameter>
</function>
</tool_call>
